title: Is Walmart Set to Win Big as Back-to-School Shopping Starts Early? WMT launches its six-day Walmart Deals event to capture early back-to-school spending with deep discounts and tech upgrades.

Analysis shows Trump's tariffs would cost US employers $82.3 billion An analysis finds that a critical group of U.S. employers would face a direct cost of $82.3 billion from President Donald Trump’s current tariff plans, a sum that could be potentially managed through price hikes, layoffs, hiring freezes or lower profit margins.  The analysis by the JPMorganChase Institute is among the first to measure the direct costs created by the import taxes on businesses with $10 million to $1 billion in annual revenue, a category that includes roughly a third of private-sector U.S. workers.  The findings show clear trade-offs from Trump’s import taxes, contradicting his claims that foreign manufacturers would absorb the costs of the tariffs instead of U.S. companies that rely on imports.

'This Is Just A Necessary Spend': Back-To-School Spending Holds Steady Despite Economic Uncertainties Summer may be just getting underway, but plenty of parents are already planning their spending for the upcoming school year. Nearly 75% of parents plan to spend the same or more on back-to-school shopping this year, a consumer survey from PwC found. And more than one in three parents actually plan to spend more this year than they have previously. Will Tariff-Induced Supply Chain Unpredictability Grinch-ify 'Christmas in July'? In last year's 48-hour window, Amazon Prime Day set a record of $14.2 billion in sales revenue. The online retail giant has increased the duration of its biggest sales event of the year to four days in 2025, running from July 8 to July 11. Walmart, Target, Best Buy, Kohl's and other retailers are attempting to get the jump on Amazon by starting their versions of "Black Friday in July" sales events on July 7, with some running past July 11.
